Stunning, gut-punch museum, even when you know much of the story. If I had to choose, I'd pick the Museum of Tolerance, probably because it's easier to take and more hopeful-- as the titles indicate. That said, the exhibits here are compelling and very well laid out. My one complaint is that while there are small pieces about the Roma and Sinti, the disabled, and the Japanese in the US, homosexuals get nothing but a cursory mention. The holocaust was devastating to them, too.
I especially appreciated the heroes section at the end, it's so important to remember those courageous people.

LOVED how this was done.  Don't shy away because it seems too heavy.  This museum did an amazing job of informing the context of the Holocaust.  Chillingly relevant today.  They focused less on the gruesome atrocities and more on the context; how the whole thing slowly built.  As you pass through chronologically, you see the front page of the LA Times for each time period.  Such a good way to lay this out. GO!!
